tasks.title: Remove or register unused queue.json->queue.jsonc migration code to avoid misleading behavior
tasks.description:
Principles violated: B (YAGNI/dead code), J (Consistency/Integrity), G (Least astonishment).

The codebase contains file-migration helpers for renaming `.ralph/queue.json` to `.ralph/queue.jsonc` (and done.json to done.jsonc), but the migration registry does not register any file rename migrations. This creates misleading documentation/comments and a latent footgun if later registered without fixing config defaults.

Evidence:
- File migration functions exist and appear 'official': `crates/ralph/src/migration/file_migrations.rs:185-206` (`migrate_queue_json_to_jsonc`, applicability checks).
- Migration registry only contains a single config-key rename migration: `crates/ralph/src/migration/registry.rs:40-47`.
- Running `ralph migrate --list` in a repo with `.ralph/queue.json` shows no file migrations; only `config_key_rename_parallel_worktree_root_2026_02` is listed.
- Default config still resolves queue path to `.ralph/queue.json`: `crates/ralph/src/config.rs:597-610`.

Why it matters:
- Contributors/users reading migration docs/comments can assume queue.json->queue.jsonc is supported and applied, but it is not.
- If this migration is registered later without also updating queue-path resolution, users on default config (no `queue.file`) could end up with a renamed file that Ralph no longer reads, appearing as an empty queue.

Minimal proposed fix:
- Pick one path and make it explicit:
  1) Delete the unused queue/done json->jsonc convenience functions + tests and remove misleading references in module docs, OR
  2) Register safe FileRename migrations and simultaneously update `resolve_queue_path`/`resolve_done_path` to auto-detect `.jsonc` when the default `.json` is missing (and document it).

Acceptance criteria:
- `ralph migrate --list` and `--apply` behavior matches what is documented (no orphaned migrations).
- If file rename migrations are registered: a repo with only `.ralph/queue.jsonc` remains readable without requiring `queue.file` in config, and `make ci` passes.
evidence: File: crates/ralph/src/migration/file_migrations.rs:185-206 defines queue.json->queue.jsonc migration helpers
File: crates/ralph/src/migration/registry.rs:40-47 registers only a config-key rename migration
File: crates/ralph/src/config.rs:597-610 defaults queue path to .ralph/queue.json
plan: Decide whether queue/done file renames are intended to be supported migrations.
If not intended: delete the queue/done json->jsonc helpers and update module docs/comments that claim this migration exists.
If intended: register FileRename migrations and update queue/done path resolution to auto-detect .jsonc when defaults are missing.
Add tests covering the chosen behavior (including default-config repos).
Verify by running `make ci`.

tasks.title: Revalidate config after applying profile patches to prevent invalid profile values
tasks.description:
Principles violated: E (Make bugs hard to write), J (Consistency/Integrity), F (Fail fast).

Config validation currently runs before applying `--profile` patches, which allows a profile to introduce invalid config values without revalidation.

Evidence:
- `crates/ralph/src/config.rs:193-198` calls `validate_config(&cfg)?;` before `apply_profile_patch(&mut cfg, name)?;`.

Why it matters:
- Profiles are a first-class feature; invalid values introduced by a profile should fail fast with actionable errors.
- Without revalidation, illegal states can reach runtime paths and cause confusing downstream failures.

Minimal proposed fix:
- After `apply_profile_patch`, re-run `validate_config(&cfg)` (or validate the patch within `apply_profile_patch`).
- Add a unit test where a custom profile sets an invalid value (e.g., `agent.iterations = 0` or `queue.id_width = 0`) and assert that resolution fails.

Acceptance criteria:
- New unit tests cover invalid profile values and confirm an error is returned during config resolution.
- `cargo test -p ralph` and `make ci` pass.
evidence: File: crates/ralph/src/config.rs:193-198 validates before applying profile patch
plan: Update config resolution to validate after applying a selected profile patch.
Add unit tests that create a profile with invalid values and assert config resolution fails.
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph` and `make ci`.

tasks.title: Stop silently ignoring invalid RFC3339 timestamps in history and burndown reports
tasks.description:
Principles violated: F (Fail fast appropriately), H (Operational hygiene), J (Consistency/Integrity).

The history and burndown reports currently ignore timestamp parse failures, which can silently drop tasks from reports and produce misleading analytics.

Evidence:
- History skips tasks with invalid timestamps by gating on `let Ok(dt) = parse_rfc3339(...)`: `crates/ralph/src/reports/history.rs:77-98`.
- Burndown treats invalid timestamps as `None` via `.ok()`: `crates/ralph/src/reports/burndown.rs:88-96`.
- Both modules document an invariant that timestamps are RFC3339 (`Invariants/assumptions` headers).

Why it matters:
- Reports can undercount created/completed tasks or remaining tasks without any signal, leading to wrong decisions based on analytics.
- Invalid timestamps should be surfaced as warnings (non-fatal) or errors (fatal), but not silently ignored.

Minimal proposed fix:
- Track timestamp parse errors and surface them:
  - Option A: return an error when invalid timestamps are encountered (strict).
  - Option B: include a warnings list in the report output (JSON and text) and log warnings (lenient).
- Add unit tests covering malformed timestamps and asserting the chosen behavior.

Acceptance criteria:
- Malformed timestamps are surfaced (warning or error) rather than silently ignored.
- Unit tests cover both history and burndown failure modes.
- `cargo test -p ralph` and `make ci` pass.
evidence: File: crates/ralph/src/reports/history.rs:77-98 silently skips invalid created_at/completed_at timestamps
File: crates/ralph/src/reports/burndown.rs:88-96 silently treats invalid timestamps as None
plan: Decide strict vs lenient behavior for invalid timestamps (error vs warning).
Implement parse-error handling consistently in both history and burndown reports.
Add unit tests with malformed timestamps to assert behavior.
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph` and `make ci`.

tasks.title: Make crates/ralph/src/lib.rs start with module docs (//! on line 1) to match repo standards
tasks.description:
Principles violated: J (Consistency/Integrity), D (Localize change).

Repo guidance requires module docs at the start of each Rust file. `crates/ralph/src/lib.rs` currently begins with an inner attribute and only introduces `//!` module docs starting at line 3.

Evidence:
- `crates/ralph/src/lib.rs:1-4` shows `#![...]` first and `//!` starts at line 3.

Why it matters:
- Consistency: contributors rely on the invariant that files begin with responsibility/non-responsibility/invariants docs for fast navigation and review.
- Tooling: simple checks for doc headers often assume first non-empty line is `//!`.

Minimal proposed fix:
- Move the `//!` header to line 1 and place `#![deny(...)]` immediately after it (standard Rust pattern).
- Add/extend a lightweight test or lint script to enforce the “first non-empty line is `//!`” rule if desired.

Acceptance criteria:
- `crates/ralph/src/lib.rs` begins with `//!` module docs on line 1.
- `cargo test -p ralph` and `make ci` pass.
evidence: File: crates/ralph/src/lib.rs:1-4 (`//!` module docs do not start the file)
plan: Move `//!` module docs to the top of `crates/ralph/src/lib.rs` (line 1).
Ensure crate-level `#![...]` attributes remain effective after the doc comment.
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph` and `make ci`.

tasks.title: Widen Makefile backup-artifact check to catch .bak files created outside crates/ralph/src
tasks.description:
Principles violated: H (Operational hygiene), J (Consistency/Integrity).

`make ci` includes a `check-backup-artifacts` step, but it only searches `crates/ralph/src/` for `*.bak` files. Release scripts create `.bak` files in other locations (e.g., `crates/ralph/Cargo.toml.bak`, temp files), and if a script fails before cleanup, these backups can be left behind unnoticed.

Evidence:
- `Makefile:116-123` searches only `crates/ralph/src/` for `*.bak`.
- `scripts/release.sh:219-221` uses `sed -i.bak` on `crates/ralph/Cargo.toml`.
- `scripts/release.sh:333-339` uses `sed -i.bak` on a temp file before cleanup.

Why it matters:
- Stray `.bak` files can be accidentally committed or confuse tooling.
- CI should fail fast when backup artifacts exist anywhere relevant, not just in one subtree.

Minimal proposed fix:
- Update `check-backup-artifacts` to scan the repo for `*.bak` (excluding `.git/` and optionally `target/`), and print actionable output.
- Consider adding `trap` cleanup in scripts that create `.bak` files so cleanup happens on failure paths.

Acceptance criteria:
- `make check-backup-artifacts` fails when a `.bak` file exists outside `crates/ralph/src/` (with clear error message).
- `make ci` still passes in a clean repo.
evidence: File: Makefile:116-123 only checks crates/ralph/src for *.bak
File: scripts/release.sh:219-221 creates Cargo.toml.bak via sed -i.bak
plan: Update `check-backup-artifacts` to search an appropriate repo-wide set of paths for `*.bak`.
Add a small test or CI contract test that creates a dummy `.bak` outside crates/ralph/src and asserts the Makefile target fails.
Verify by running `make check-backup-artifacts` and `make ci`.

tasks.title: Fix generate-changelog.sh help examples: add missing Makefile targets or remove misleading references
tasks.description:
Principles violated: J (Consistency/Integrity), G (Least astonishment).

`scripts/generate-changelog.sh --help` advertises `make changelog` and `make changelog-preview`, but the Makefile does not define those targets.

Evidence:
- Help text includes Makefile targets: `scripts/generate-changelog.sh:295-300`.
- `Makefile` contains no `changelog` targets (`rg -n changelog Makefile` finds nothing).

Why it matters:
- Users following `--help` instructions hit dead ends, harming UX and trust.

Minimal proposed fix:
- Either add Makefile targets:
  - `changelog`: runs `scripts/generate-changelog.sh`
  - `changelog-preview`: runs `scripts/generate-changelog.sh --dry-run`
- Or remove the Makefile examples from `--help` output and keep script-only examples.

Acceptance criteria:
- `scripts/generate-changelog.sh --help` examples are correct.
- If Makefile targets are added: `make changelog-preview` runs successfully and does not modify CHANGELOG.md.
evidence: File: scripts/generate-changelog.sh:295-300 references make changelog targets
Command: rg -n changelog Makefile (no matches)
plan: Decide whether to add Makefile targets or adjust script help text.
Implement the chosen fix (targets and/or help text changes).
Add/update an existing help-output contract test if needed.
Verify by running `scripts/generate-changelog.sh --help` and (if applicable) `make changelog-preview`.

tasks.title: Make release artifact builds reproducible: use cargo build --locked in build-release-artifacts.sh
tasks.description:
Principles violated: I (Security baseline), H (Operational hygiene), J (Consistency/Integrity).

Release artifact builds should be reproducible and pinned to `Cargo.lock`. `scripts/build-release-artifacts.sh` currently uses `cargo build --release` without `--locked`, which can allow dependency resolution drift during release builds.

Evidence:
- `scripts/build-release-artifacts.sh:115-117` runs `cargo build --release -p ralph --target ...` with no `--locked`.

Why it matters:
- Release binaries may be built against unintended dependency versions, harming reproducibility and increasing supply-chain risk.

Minimal proposed fix:
- Add `--locked` to cargo invocations in `scripts/build-release-artifacts.sh`.
- Ensure error messaging remains actionable if Cargo.lock is out of date.

Acceptance criteria:
- `scripts/build-release-artifacts.sh --help` still works and examples remain valid.
- A local dry run of the script (current-target path) succeeds using `--locked`.
- `make ci` passes.
evidence: File: scripts/build-release-artifacts.sh:115-117 cargo build without --locked
plan: Add `--locked` to cargo build invocations in `scripts/build-release-artifacts.sh`.
Run `scripts/build-release-artifacts.sh --help` and (optionally) a current-platform build to validate behavior.
Verify by running `make ci`.

tasks.title: Centralize queue ID prefix/width validation to avoid drift between config and queue validation
tasks.description:
Principles violated: C (DRY), J (Consistency/Integrity).

Queue ID prefix/width validation rules are duplicated across config resolution and queue validation, increasing drift risk (different error messages or constraints over time).

Evidence:
- Config validation: `crates/ralph/src/config.rs:586-595` validates and resolves `queue.id_prefix` and `queue.id_width`.
- Queue validation: `crates/ralph/src/queue/validation.rs:64-75` independently validates `id_width` and `id_prefix`.

Why it matters:
- Users can get inconsistent behavior/errors depending on whether a code path hits config validation first vs queue validation.
- Maintaining one canonical set of rules reduces defect risk.

Minimal proposed fix:
- Move ID prefix/width validation into a shared helper (e.g., `queue::validation::{validate_id_prefix, validate_id_width}`) and call it from both config and queue validation.
- Add unit tests asserting a single canonical error message for invalid prefix/width.

Acceptance criteria:
- One source of truth for ID prefix/width validation is used by both config resolution and queue validation.
- Unit tests cover invalid values (empty prefix, width 0).
- `cargo test -p ralph` and `make ci` pass.
evidence: File: crates/ralph/src/config.rs:586-595 validates id_prefix/id_width
File: crates/ralph/src/queue/validation.rs:64-75 re-validates id_prefix/id_width
plan: Extract shared ID prefix/width validation helpers.
Replace duplicated logic in config resolution and queue validation with calls to the shared helpers.
Add unit tests to lock error messages and behavior.
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph` and `make ci`.

tasks.title: Reuse config validation in TUI config editor to keep error rules/messages consistent
tasks.description:
Principles violated: C (DRY), G (Least astonishment), J (Consistency/Integrity).

The TUI config editor enforces certain validations (e.g., `queue.id_width > 0`, `agent.iterations > 0`) with bespoke error messages, duplicating `crates/ralph/src/config.rs` validation rules.

Evidence:
- TUI validation snippets: `crates/ralph/src/tui/config_edit.rs:450-481` enforce `queue.id_width` and `agent.iterations` constraints.
- Config validation rules live elsewhere and can drift: `crates/ralph/src/config.rs:586-595` and other config validation helpers.

Why it matters:
- Users get inconsistent validation behavior and error messages between CLI config validation and TUI editing.
- Duplicated rule implementations increase maintenance burden and bug risk.

Minimal proposed fix:
- Extract shared validators (or reuse existing ones) so both config resolution and TUI editing share a single source of truth.
- Add unit tests covering TUI edit attempts for invalid values and asserting consistent messages/behavior.

Acceptance criteria:
- `queue.id_width` and `agent.iterations` validation rules are shared (not duplicated).
- TUI unit tests verify invalid inputs return the expected errors.
- `cargo test -p ralph` and `make ci` pass.
evidence: File: crates/ralph/src/tui/config_edit.rs:450-481 validates queue.id_width and agent.iterations
File: crates/ralph/src/config.rs:586-595 validates id_prefix/id_width in config resolution
plan: Identify and extract shared validation functions for the edited config keys.
Update TUI config editor to call shared validators and reuse canonical error messages.
Add unit tests covering invalid TUI edits for queue.id_width and agent.iterations.
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph` and `make ci`.

tasks.title: Add dedicated migrations documentation page and link it from docs index and CLI reference
tasks.description:
Principles violated: J (Consistency/Integrity), H (Operational hygiene).

Migrations are a core workflow feature (`ralph migrate`) but there is no dedicated documentation page explaining migration types, safety/backups, history tracking, and recovery.

Evidence:
- `docs/` contains no `migrations.md` (see `ls docs`).
- `docs/index.md:7-17` does not link any migration guide, despite `ralph migrate` being a core command.

Why it matters:
- Users and contributors lack a single source of truth for what migrations do and how to apply/rollback safely.
- Increases support burden and risk of incorrect usage (e.g., running with `--force` without understanding).

Minimal proposed fix:
- Create `docs/migrations.md` covering:
  - when migrations are checked
  - how to list/check/apply
  - `--auto-fix` behavior
  - backup/history files (paths, invariants)
  - examples for common migration scenarios
- Link it from `docs/index.md` and cross-link from `docs/cli.md` migrate section.

Acceptance criteria:
- `docs/migrations.md` exists and includes runnable CLI examples.
evidence: File: docs/index.md:7-17 has no migration doc link
Command: ls docs (no docs/migrations.md present)
plan: Create `docs/migrations.md` describing migration system behavior, safety, and examples.
Add links from `docs/index.md` and relevant sections of `docs/cli.md`.
Verify by checking links resolve (relative paths) and running `rg -n migrations.md docs/index.md docs/cli.md`.

tasks.title: Resolve clippy vec-init-then-push suggestion in daemon_integration_test temp dir selection
tasks.description:
Principles violated: A (KISS), J (Consistency/Integrity).

Clippy emits `clippy::vec-init-then-push` for `crates/ralph/tests/daemon_integration_test.rs`, suggesting the code should use `vec![..]` initialization. While this does not currently fail the lint step, it is noisy and can become a hard failure if lint configuration changes.

Evidence:
- `make ci` clippy output reported:
  - warning: calls to `push` immediately after creation
  - location: `crates/ralph/tests/daemon_integration_test.rs:48-50`
- Source: `crates/ralph/tests/daemon_integration_test.rs:48-51` uses `let mut candidates: Vec<PathBuf> = Vec::new();` then pushes.

Why it matters:
- Keeps CI/lint output clean and reduces future churn.

Minimal proposed fix:
- Replace with `let candidates: Vec<PathBuf> = vec![std::env::temp_dir(), PathBuf::from("/tmp")];` and iterate.

Acceptance criteria:
- `make lint` emits no warning for `clippy::vec-init-then-push` in that test.
- `make ci` passes.
evidence: File: crates/ralph/tests/daemon_integration_test.rs:48-51 (vec init then push)
make ci clippy output: clippy::vec-init-then-push at crates/ralph/tests/daemon_integration_test.rs:48-50
plan: Apply the `vec![..]` initialization refactor in `crates/ralph/tests/daemon_integration_test.rs`.
Run `make lint` to confirm clippy warning is gone.
Verify by running `make ci`.

tasks.title: Add unit tests for core run loop execution (commands/run/)
tasks.description:
The core run loop and execution logic in commands/run/ lacks unit test coverage. This is the highest-risk module as bugs here affect all task runs. Files like run_loop.rs (736 lines), run_one/orchestration.rs (991 lines), and all phase files (phase1.rs, phase2.rs, phase3.rs, single.rs) have zero unit tests despite being critical execution paths.

Goals:
1. Add unit tests for run_loop.rs covering normal execution, error handling, and interruption
2. Add tests for orchestration.rs covering task preparation, runner invocation, and result handling
3. Add tests for each phase (phase1, phase2, phase3, single) covering success and failure paths
4. Mock runner execution for isolated testing
5. Test session management and cleanup
evidence: File: crates/ralph/src/commands/run/run_loop.rs - 736 lines, 0 unit tests
File: crates/ralph/src/commands/run/run_one/orchestration.rs - 991 lines, 0 unit tests
File: crates/ralph/src/commands/run/phases/phase1.rs - 445 lines, 0 unit tests
File: crates/ralph/src/commands/run/phases/phase2.rs - 458 lines, 0 unit tests
File: crates/ralph/src/commands/run/phases/phase3.rs - 515 lines, 0 unit tests
Module: commands/run/ contains core execution logic - bugs affect all task runs
CONTRIBUTING.md requires: 'All new or changed behavior must be covered by tests'
plan: Create test module in run_loop.rs with #[cfg(test)] covering: execute_next_task(), handle_interrupt(), cleanup_on_exit()
Add tests for orchestration.rs: prepare_context(), invoke_runner(), parse_result(), handle_failure()
Add tests for phase1.rs: plan generation, phase transitions, error handling
Add tests for phase2.rs: implementation execution, checkpoint saving, continuation logic
Add tests for phase3.rs: review execution, completion signaling, result verification
Create mock runner for isolated testing without external dependencies
Test session ID generation format: {task_id}-p{phase}-{timestamp}
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph commands::run` and confirming coverage
notes: Priority: High - Core execution path with zero test coverage

tasks.title: Add unit tests for queue operations (queue/)
tasks.description:
The queue module contains the data foundation for all task operations. Corruption here is catastrophic. Files in queue/operations/, queue/graph/, and queue/search/ lack unit tests despite handling critical data operations like task mutations, graph algorithms, and search functionality.

Goals:
1. Add tests for queue operations: query.rs, mutation.rs, status.rs, archive.rs
2. Add tests for graph operations: algorithms.rs, build.rs, traversal.rs
3. Add tests for search operations: substring.rs, normalize.rs, options.rs
4. Test edge cases: empty queues, malformed data, concurrent modifications
5. Test validation logic in validation.rs
evidence: File: crates/ralph/src/queue/operations/ - All 7 files lack unit tests
File: crates/ralph/src/queue/graph/ - All 4 files lack unit tests
File: crates/ralph/src/queue/search/ - All 5 files lack unit tests
Module: queue/ is data foundation - queue.json is source of truth
AGENTS.md: 'Queue is the source of truth' - data corruption is catastrophic
50% of source files (210 of 418) lack unit tests
plan: Add tests for query.rs: find_task(), next_todo_task(), next_runnable_task(), are_dependencies_met()
Add tests for mutation.rs: sort_tasks_by_priority(), backfill_missing_fields(), split_task()
Add tests for status.rs: set_status(), complete_task(), promote_draft_to_todo()
Add tests for archive.rs: archive_completed(), restore_archived()
Add tests for graph algorithms: dependency resolution, cycle detection, topological sort
Add tests for search: substring matching, fuzzy search, regex search
Test validation: validate_queue(), validate_queue_set(), handle malformed JSON
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph queue::` and checking coverage report
notes: Priority: High - Data foundation with zero test coverage

tasks.title: Add unit tests for runner execution system
tasks.description:
The runner module handles process management, output streaming, and runner configuration. This module manages external process execution and has zero unit tests despite the high risk of race conditions, I/O errors, and process management bugs.

Goals:
1. Add tests for runner/execution/stream.rs (759 lines) - output streaming logic
2. Add tests for runner/execution/json.rs - JSON result parsing
3. Add tests for runner/execution/runners.rs - runner management
4. Add tests for runner/settings.rs - runner configuration
5. Test error handling: process failures, timeouts, invalid JSON, stream errors

File: crates/ralph/src/runner/execution/json.rs - 0 unit tests
File: crates/ralph/src/runner/execution/runners.rs - 0 unit tests
Module: runner/ handles external process execution - high risk area
12 files in runner module lack unit tests
Runner execution is critical path for all AI agent interactions
plan: Add tests for stream.rs: output capture, line buffering, stream merging
Add tests for json.rs: valid result parsing, malformed JSON handling, schema validation
Add tests for runners.rs: runner discovery, selection, priority handling
Add tests for settings.rs: config loading, validation, default handling
Test error scenarios: process timeout, non-zero exit codes, broken pipes
Test streaming edge cases: empty output, very long lines, binary data
Mock external runners for isolated testing
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph runner::` and confirming coverage
notes: Priority: High - Process management with zero test coverage

tasks.title: Fix timing-dependent flaky tests in lock_race_cleanup_test.rs
tasks.description:
The lock_race_cleanup_test.rs file contains multiple tests that rely on fixed-duration thread::sleep() calls for synchronization. This is an anti-pattern that causes flaky tests on slower/faster systems. Tests like test_rapid_acquire_release_no_leak use 2ms sleep x 50 cycles which may fail under load.

Goals:
1. Replace thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(50/100)) with polling + timeout pattern
2. Fix test_rapid_acquire_release_no_leak - remove hardcoded 2ms sleeps
3. Ensure tests are deterministic and pass consistently across different system loads
4. Maintain test coverage while improving reliability
evidence: File: crates/ralph/tests/lock_race_cleanup_test.rs lines 50, 94, 100, 117, 129, 143, 174, 186
Code: thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(50)) // Give a moment for pending cleanup
Code: thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(2)) x 50 cycles in test_rapid_acquire_release_no_leak
Anti-pattern: Fixed sleep durations assume specific timing that varies by system load
Risk: Tests may pass locally but fail in CI or on different hardware
plan: Replace fixed sleeps with polling pattern: while !condition && timeout_not_reached { sleep(short_interval) }
Define constants: POLL_INTERVAL_MS = 10, MAX_WAIT_MS = 5000
Update test_lock_released_on_process_exit: poll for lock release instead of fixed 100ms sleep
Update test_rapid_acquire_release_no_leak: use barrier or atomic counter instead of timing
Update test_lock_auto_expires_after_ttl: verify via polling, not fixed sleep
Run tests 10 times in a row: for i in {1..10}; do cargo test -p ralph lock_race; done
Verify all tests pass consistently without flakiness
notes: Priority: High - Flaky tests reduce CI reliability and developer trust

tasks.title: Fix timing-dependent flaky tests in webhook_integration_test.rs
tasks.description:
The webhook_integration_test.rs file uses fixed-duration thread::sleep() calls (200ms-2000ms) to wait for async operations. This causes flaky tests and unnecessarily slow test execution. Some tests also have weak assertions that only verify 'no panic occurred' rather than actual behavior.

Goals:
1. Replace fixed sleeps (200ms, 500ms, 1500ms) with polling + timeout pattern
2. Strengthen weak assertions in webhook_retries_failed_deliveries, webhook_drop_new_policy, webhook_drop_oldest_policy
3. Reduce test execution time while maintaining reliability
4. Add proper verification of retry counts, drop behavior, and queue state
evidence: File: crates/ralph/tests/webhook_integration_test.rs lines 194, 310, 418, 458
Code: thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(500)) // Wait for processing (including retry)
Code: thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(1500)) // Give time for processing
Weak assertion: 'Test passes if no panic occurred (retry logic was exercised)'
Weak assertion: 'Test passes if no panic occurred' for drop policy tests
No verification of actual retry count, drop behavior, or queue state
plan: Replace thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(500)) with polling loop checking actual condition
Replace thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(1500)) with event-based synchronization or polling
Strengthen webhook_retries_failed_deliveries: verify request count equals retry_count + 1
Strengthen webhook_drop_new_policy: count accepted vs dropped webhooks
Strengthen webhook_drop_oldest_policy: verify oldest items were dropped
Add webhook delivery state inspection for deterministic assertions
Run tests 10 times to verify no flakiness
Measure test execution time before/after optimization
notes: Priority: High - Flaky tests and weak assertions reduce confidence

tasks.title: Consolidate duplicate test helper functions across test files
tasks.description:
Multiple integration test files contain duplicate helper functions that already exist in test_support.rs. This creates maintenance overhead and inconsistency. Files like daemon_integration_test.rs and safeguard_integration_test.rs duplicate functions for git setup, CLI execution, and runner configuration.

Goals:
1. Remove duplicate ralph_bin() from daemon_integration_test.rs - use test_support::ralph_bin()
2. Remove duplicate temp_dir_outside_repo() from daemon_integration_test.rs - use test_support version
3. Remove duplicate git_init(), configure_runner(), create_fake_runner() from safeguard_integration_test.rs
4. Ensure all integration tests use shared helpers from test_support.rs
5. Document the test_support module pattern for future tests

tasks.title: Install and configure cargo-llvm-cov for test coverage reporting
tasks.description:
The project currently lacks code coverage tooling. cargo-llvm-cov is not installed, making it impossible to measure test coverage or identify untested code paths. This is essential for prioritizing test additions and tracking coverage improvements.

Goals:
1. Install cargo-llvm-cov: cargo install cargo-llvm-cov
2. Add coverage target to Makefile: make coverage
3. Configure coverage thresholds and exclusions (target/, tests/, generated code)
4. Add coverage report generation (HTML and LCOV formats)
5. Document how to run coverage locally
6. Optionally add coverage badge to README.md

CONTRIBUTING.md: No coverage requirements documented
Finding: 50% of source files (210 of 418) lack unit tests - need coverage data to prioritize
Finding: 34.4% of lines (48,393 of 140,558) are not covered by tests
plan: Install cargo-llvm-cov: cargo install cargo-llvm-cov
Add 'coverage' target to Makefile with: cargo llvm-cov --workspace --html --output-dir target/coverage
Add 'coverage-lcov' target for IDE integration: cargo llvm-cov --workspace --lcov --output-path target/coverage.lcov
Configure exclusions in Cargo.toml: exclude files in target/, tests/, generated/ from coverage
Document in CONTRIBUTING.md: 'Run make coverage before submitting PR to check test coverage'
Add .codecov.yml or similar config if external coverage service desired
Verify by running make coverage and viewing target/coverage/html/index.html
Verify coverage data shows current baseline (~65% line coverage)
notes: Priority: Medium - Essential for measuring and improving test coverage

tasks.title: Add nextest configuration for faster test execution
tasks.description:
The project currently uses default cargo test which runs tests sequentially. Adding nextest configuration would enable parallel test execution, retries for flaky tests, and better test output formatting. This would significantly reduce CI time and improve developer experience.

Goals:
1. Install cargo-nextest: cargo install cargo-nextest
2. Create nextest.toml configuration file
3. Configure test profiles: default (parallel), ci (retries, fail-fast), local (fast feedback)
4. Update Makefile to use nextest for test and ci targets
5. Configure retries for known flaky tests
6. Document nextest usage in CONTRIBUTING.md
evidence: Current: cargo test runs sequentially - slow for 73+ integration tests
Missing: No nextest.toml configuration file
Makefile: Uses 'cargo test' instead of 'cargo nextest run'
Opportunity: Tests are mostly independent and can run in parallel
Serial tests already marked: webhook tests use #[serial] where needed
Estimated speedup: 3-5x faster test execution on multi-core systems
plan: Install cargo-nextest: cargo install cargo-nextest --locked
Create nextest.toml with profiles:
[profile.default] test-threads = 'num-cpus' retries = 0
[profile.ci] test-threads = 'num-cpus' retries = 2 fail-fast = true
[profile.local] test-threads = 'num-cpus' retries = 0 fail-fast = false
Update Makefile 'test' target: replace cargo test with cargo nextest run --profile ci
Update Makefile 'test-unit' target for local development without coverage
Add 'test-local' target for quick iteration: cargo nextest run --profile local
Document in CONTRIBUTING.md: 'Use make test-local for quick feedback during development'
Verify by running make test and measuring execution time improvement
Ensure all tests pass with nextest (check for test interdependencies)
notes: Priority: Medium - Significant CI speed improvement

tasks.title: Add missing error case coverage for queue_next_test.rs
tasks.description:
The queue_next_test.rs file has only one test case and lacks error scenarios. It should test corrupted queues, missing files, and invalid task states to ensure robust error handling when selecting the next task.

Goals:
1. Add test for corrupted queue.json (malformed JSON)
2. Add test for missing queue file
3. Add test when no tasks are runnable (all blocked, done, or draft)
4. Add test for cyclic dependencies (no valid next task)
5. Add test for empty queue
6. Verify error messages are helpful and actionable
evidence: File: crates/ralph/tests/queue_next_test.rs - Only one test case
Missing: No error case coverage
Command tested: ralph queue next - critical for workflow
Risk: Error conditions may panic or produce unhelpful messages
AGENTS.md: 'Testing guidelines: cover success + failure modes'
plan: Add test_corrupted_queue: Malformed JSON returns error, not panic
Add test_missing_queue_file: File not found returns clear error
Add test_no_runnable_tasks: All tasks blocked/done, verify empty result
Add test_cyclic_dependencies: Circular deps detected, error reported
Add test_empty_queue: No tasks in queue, verify empty result
Add test_all_draft_tasks: --include-draft required, verify behavior
Verify error messages include actionable guidance
Run cargo test -p ralph queue_next to verify all tests
Ensure make ci passes with new tests
notes: Priority: Low - Error case coverage for queue next command

tasks.title: Add tests for task relations CLI commands
tasks.description:
The task relations CLI commands in cli/task/relations.rs handle adding, removing, and querying task dependencies but have no unit tests. These commands are critical for task dependency management and bugs can corrupt the task graph.

Goals:
1. Add tests for task relations add - add dependencies between tasks
2. Add tests for task relations remove - remove dependencies
3. Add tests for task relations list - show dependencies of a task
4. Add tests for cycle detection when adding relations
5. Test error handling: non-existent tasks, self-references, duplicate relations
6. Test with task_relations_cli_test.rs patterns
evidence: File: crates/ralph/src/cli/task/relations.rs - No unit tests
Integration tests exist: task_relations_cli_test.rs (218 lines)
Gap: No unit tests for relations logic
Critical: Relations affect task execution order
Risk: Cycle detection bugs can cause infinite loops
plan: Add unit tests for relations.rs: add_dependency(), remove_dependency()
Test cycle detection: Adding A->B->C->A should fail
Test self-reference: Adding A->A should fail
Test duplicate relation: Adding same relation twice should be idempotent
Test non-existent task: Referencing unknown task ID should error
Use test_support for creating task fixtures with relations
Verify integration tests still pass: cargo test task_relations_cli
Verify by running `cargo test -p ralph relations` and checking coverage
notes: Priority: Low - Task relations command testing

tasks.title: Add ETA accuracy tracking and prediction improvement
tasks.description:
ETA estimates are provided but never validated against actual completion times. No feedback loop to improve predictions. Add accuracy tracking to measure and improve ETA predictions over time.

Goals:
1. Track predicted vs actual completion times for each task
2. Calculate ETA accuracy metrics (MAE, bias, variance by runner/model)
3. Add `ralph productivity accuracy` command showing prediction performance
4. Use accuracy data to adjust future predictions (calibration)
5. Show confidence intervals, not just point estimates

Evidence:
- Current: ETA calculator exists but only computes weighted averages
- Gap: No accuracy measurement or feedback loop
- User pain: ETAs may be consistently optimistic/pessimistic but never corrected
evidence: Current capability: ETA calculator computes weighted averages by (runner, model, phase_count)
Gap observed: No comparison of predicted vs actual times - predictions never improve
Execution history: Stores phase durations but not the ETA that was predicted
Confidence levels: High/Medium/Low exist but based only on sample count, not accuracy
plan: Add predicted_duration_ms to execution history entries (captured at task start)
Add accuracy metrics to productivity data: mae_ms, bias_ms, variance_ms by (runner, model)
Implement accuracy calculation: compare predicted vs actual at task completion
Add calibration factor that adjusts future predictions based on historical bias
Add `ralph productivity accuracy [--runner X] [--days 30]` command
Show confidence intervals in TUI: 'ETA: 5-8 minutes (85% confidence)'
Add accuracy webhook events for external monitoring
Update docs with ETA accuracy interpretation guide
Add tests for calibration algorithm edge cases (insufficient data, outliers)
Verify by running `ralph productivity accuracy` after completing several tasks and seeing metrics

custom_fields.user_value: Improves planning accuracy, builds trust in ETAs, enables better time management

tasks.title: Add AI-powered task complexity analysis with auto-split suggestions
tasks.description:
No automated assistance for determining if a task is too large or should be split. Users must manually judge complexity. Add AI analysis that evaluates task scope/plan and suggests splitting when appropriate.

Goals:
1. Add `ralph task analyze <TASK_ID>` command for complexity evaluation
2. Analyze: scope length, plan step count, evidence detail, estimated effort
3. Suggest splitting when complexity exceeds thresholds
4. Generate split proposals with logical task boundaries
5. Integrate with existing `ralph task split` command

Evidence:
- Current: Manual task splitting exists (`ralph task split`) but no guidance on when to use
- Gap: No complexity heuristics or automated analysis
- User pain: Large tasks often fail or take much longer than expected
evidence: Current capability: `ralph task split` exists but is manual - no guidance on when to split
Gap observed: No complexity scoring or automated suggestions
Refactor task builder: Exists for large files but not for task complexity
Task schema: Has scope, evidence, plan fields that could be analyzed
plan: Create TaskComplexityAnalyzer with heuristics: scope word count, plan step count, evidence quality
Add complexity_score (1-10) and complexity_reasoning to task custom_fields
Implement `ralph task analyze <TASK_ID> [--suggest-split]` command
Add --analyze flag to `ralph task build` to check complexity before creating
Integrate with split command: `ralph task split --auto-suggest` uses AI to propose boundaries
Add complexity warning in TUI when selecting high-complexity tasks
Add webhook event for complexity_analysis_complete
Update task builder prompt to encourage right-sized tasks
Add tests for complexity scoring edge cases (short scope but complex plan)
Verify by running `ralph task analyze RQ-XXXX` on a large task and receiving split suggestions

custom_fields.user_value: Prevents oversized tasks, improves success rate, reduces manual judgment

tasks.title: Implement incoming webhook server for external integrations
tasks.description:
Ralph can SEND webhooks but cannot RECEIVE them. No HTTP endpoint for external services (GitHub, Slack, CI/CD) to trigger actions. Add an incoming webhook server for external integration.

Goals:
1. Add `ralph server` command that starts HTTP server on configurable port (default 8473)
2. Support endpoints: POST /webhook/task (create task), POST /webhook/trigger (start run)
3. Add HMAC-SHA256 signature verification for security
4. Support GitHub webhook format for issue/PR events
5. Add request queuing and rate limiting

Evidence:
- Current: Outbound webhooks exist (9 event types) but no inbound
- Gap: No HTTP server capability
- User pain: Can't integrate with external CI systems, GitHub webhooks, Slack commands
evidence: Current capability: Outbound webhook system is mature (9 events, retries, HMAC)
Gap observed: No incoming webhook capability - can't receive external triggers
No HTTP server: Main is purely CLI, no server modules exist
Integration gap: GitHub webhooks, Slack commands, CI triggers not possible
plan: Add axum or warp dependency for HTTP server (lightweight, async)
Create server module with configurable bind address and port (default 127.0.0.1:8473)
Implement POST /webhook/task endpoint for external task creation
Implement POST /webhook/trigger endpoint to start run loop
Add HMAC-SHA256 verification using configured webhook.secret
Add GitHub-compatible endpoint at /webhook/github for PR/issue events
Implement request queue with rate limiting (requests per minute per source)
Add server logging and request/response logging to .ralph/logs/server.log
Add `ralph server start/stop/status` CLI commands
Create example integrations for GitHub webhooks and Slack commands
Verify by running `ralph server start`, then POST to /webhook/task and confirming task created

custom_fields.user_value: Enables external integrations, CI triggers, GitHub/Slack commands, third-party automation

tasks.title: Add smart scheduling rules for time-based task automation
tasks.description:
No time-based automation rules. Can't automatically escalate priority, reject stale drafts, or change status based on age. Add configurable rules for time-based task actions.

Goals:
1. Add `automation_rules` config section with time-based triggers
2. Support rules: escalate priority after N days, auto-reject stale drafts, remind after delay
3. Add `ralph queue rules` command to list/apply rules
4. Support dry-run mode to preview rule effects
5. Integrate with continuous/daemon mode for automatic application

Evidence:
- Current: Basic `scheduled_start` field exists but no rule engine
- Gap: No automation based on task age or time in status
- User pain: Stale drafts accumulate, high-priority work gets forgotten
evidence: Current capability: `ralph task schedule` sets future start date - manual only
Gap observed: No recurring or conditional automation based on task state
Queue prune: Exists for age-based deletion but not general rules
Aging report: Would identify stale tasks but not act on them
plan: Add AutomationRule struct with trigger (time_in_status, age), condition, and action
Add automation_rules: Vec<AutomationRule> to config
Implement rule engine that evaluates rules against queue on schedule/trigger
Add `ralph queue rules list/test/apply` CLI commands
Support actions: set_priority, set_status, add_tag, add_note, notify
Add built-in rule templates: 'escalate_after_7d', 'reject_stale_drafts'
Integrate with daemon mode for automatic rule application
Add webhook events for rule_triggered and rule_action_applied
Add TUI notification when rules are applied to tasks
Add tests for rule evaluation including complex conditions
Verify by configuring a rule 'after 1d in draft, add tag stale', waiting 1 day, and confirming tag added

custom_fields.user_value: Prevents forgotten work, automates queue hygiene, reduces manual status management

tasks.title: Add plugin marketplace with remote install and discovery
tasks.description:
Plugins can only be installed from local directories. No discovery, versioning, or remote installation. Add a plugin marketplace for easy sharing and installation.

Goals:
1. Add `ralph plugin search <query>` to find plugins from registry
2. Add `ralph plugin install <source>` supporting git URLs, GitHub repos, registry IDs
3. Add version resolution and update checking
4. Add plugin registry format (JSON index)
5. Support signed plugins for security

Evidence:
- Current: `ralph plugin install <local-dir>` only supports local paths
- Gap: No remote installation, no discovery mechanism
- User pain: Must manually download/clone plugins, no version management
evidence: Current capability: Plugin install only supports local directory paths (commands/plugin/mod.rs)
Gap observed: No remote install, no version management, no discovery
Registry exists: PluginRegistry but only for discovered local plugins
Discovery: Only scans ~/.config/ralph/plugins/ and .ralph/plugins/
plan: Add PluginMarketplace client for fetching plugin index from registry URL
Extend `ralph plugin install` to support: github:user/repo, https://git..., registry:plugin-id
Add version resolution using semver - install latest compatible or specific version
Add `ralph plugin update [plugin-id]` to check and apply updates
Add `ralph plugin search <query>` with filtering by type (runner/processor)
Add `ralph plugin info <plugin-id>` showing description, versions, dependencies
Support plugin signing/verification with GPG or Sigstore
Add official Ralph plugin registry (GitHub repo with index.json)
Update docs with plugin publishing guide
Add tests for version resolution and update checking
Verify by running `ralph plugin install github:ralph-plugins/slack-integration` and confirming installation

custom_fields.user_value: Enables plugin ecosystem growth, simplifies installation, improves discoverability

tasks.title: Implement bidirectional GitHub sync for issues and PRs
tasks.description:
GitHub integration is one-way only (export/publish). Tasks can become GitHub issues but status doesn't sync back. Add bidirectional synchronization.

Goals:
1. Add `ralph queue sync github` command to sync task status with linked issues/PRs
2. Poll GitHub for status changes (issue closed → task done, PR merged → task done)
3. Sync comments bidirectionally
4. Support labels sync (GitHub labels ↔ tags)
5. Add webhook support for real-time sync (with ralph server feature)

Evidence:
- Current: `ralph queue issue publish` creates GitHub issues but no sync
- Gap: One-way only - no status sync back from GitHub
- User pain: Must manually mark tasks done when GitHub issues close
evidence: Current capability: `ralph queue issue publish` and parallel mode PR creation exist
Gap observed: No synchronization - GitHub issue close doesn't update task status
Export only: Export formats include GitHub markdown but no import/sync
Git module: issue.rs and pr.rs exist but only have create/update, no sync polling
plan: Add github_issue_id and github_pr_id fields to task custom_fields for linking
Implement GitHub sync polling using gh CLI or GraphQL API
Add `ralph queue sync github [--poll-interval 5m]` command
Implement status mapping: issue closed → task done, PR merged → task done
Add bidirectional comment sync (GitHub comments → task notes, task notes → GitHub comments)
Add label/tag sync configuration (map GitHub labels to Ralph tags)
Integrate with ralph server for webhook-based real-time sync
Add sync status indicator in TUI showing last sync time
Add dry-run mode to preview sync changes
Update docs with GitHub sync setup and configuration
Add tests for sync logic including conflict resolution
Verify by publishing task to GitHub, closing issue, running sync, and confirming task marked done

custom_fields.user_value: Eliminates manual status sync, keeps GitHub and Ralph in sync, enables hybrid workflows

tasks.title: Add health check system with disk space and resource monitoring
tasks.description:
No proactive health monitoring. Queue operations, temp files, and debug logs can consume disk space without warning. Add health checks that warn before resources are exhausted.

Goals:
1. Add pre-flight health checks: disk space, memory, git state, runner availability
2. Add `ralph doctor --health-check` for continuous monitoring
3. Configurable thresholds: disk_space_threshold_mb (default 1000)
4. Add health status to TUI status bar
5. Add webhook alerts for health issues

Evidence:
- Current: `ralph doctor` runs one-time checks but no continuous monitoring
- Gap: No resource monitoring or alerts
- Risk: Disk full during long runs can cause data corruption
evidence: Current capability: `ralph doctor` validates environment and runner binaries
Gap observed: No disk space, memory, or resource monitoring
Debug logs: Can grow large (.ralph/logs/debug.log) with no rotation based on disk space
Temp files: Parallel mode creates workspaces with no cleanup on disk full
plan: Add HealthChecker struct with checks: disk_space, memory, git_state, runner_available
Add health config to agent: disk_space_threshold_mb (1000), memory_threshold_percent (90)
Add pre-flight health check before task execution with warning/abort on failure
Add `ralph doctor --watch [--interval 60]` for continuous monitoring mode
Add health indicator to TUI status bar (green/yellow/red based on checks)
Add health_check_failed webhook event with details
Add automatic cleanup of old debug logs when disk space low
Add health check results to safeguard dumps for debugging
Update docs with health check configuration and troubleshooting
Add tests for health check logic including edge cases (disk full, permission denied)
Verify by running `ralph doctor --watch` with artificially low threshold and confirming alerts

custom_fields.user_value: Prevents failures due to resource exhaustion, enables proactive maintenance, improves reliability

tasks.title: Add undo/redo system for task operations
tasks.description:
No way to undo task operations. Accidental deletes, status changes, or edits can't be easily reverted. Add an undo/redo system for queue mutations.

Goals:
1. Add operation history stack for queue mutations
2. Support undo for: delete, archive, status change, edit, batch operations
3. Add `ralph queue undo` and `ralph queue redo` commands
4. Add undo support in TUI (Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Y)
5. Persist undo history across sessions (configurable)

Evidence:
- Current: No undo capability - operations are immediate and permanent
- Gap: Accidental changes require manual repair or queue.json editing
- User pain: One wrong `ralph task delete` or batch operation can lose work
evidence: Current capability: Queue repair exists for corruption but not for user mistakes
Gap observed: No operation history or undo capability anywhere
Batch operations: Can affect many tasks at once with no recovery path
Delete: `ralph task delete` immediately removes task from queue
plan: Add OperationRecord struct capturing operation type, before/after state, timestamp
Add undo_history: Vec<OperationRecord> to queue with max size limit (default 100)
Instrument all queue mutation operations to push to undo stack
Implement `ralph queue undo [--steps N]` to revert last N operations
Implement `ralph queue redo` to re-apply undone operations
Add Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Y keybindings in TUI for undo/redo
Add undo indicator in TUI showing 'Undo available (3 operations)'
Persist undo history to .ralph/cache/undo_history.json (optional, default on)
Add config option: max_undo_history_size, persist_undo_history
Update docs with undo/redo usage and limitations
Add tests for undo/redo including complex sequences and edge cases
Verify by running `ralph task delete RQ-XXXX`, then `ralph queue undo`, and confirming task restored

custom_fields.user_value: Reduces fear of mistakes, enables experimentation, improves data safety
